name: Orgone Cloudbusters
description: Orgone Cloudbusters are devices popularized within the Orgone energy movement, primarily in the United States, intended to mitigate the perceived negative effects of electromagnetic radiation and atmospheric pollution, which are believed to be forms of "deadly orgone" (DOR). Proponents claim these devices, typically constructed from metal pipes and crystals embedded in resin, can transform DOR into positive orgone energy (POR), leading to improved weather patterns, plant growth, and human health. The theory stems from the work of Wilhelm Reich, who developed the concept of orgone energy in the mid-20th century. Reich believed orgone was a universal life force that could be harnessed for various purposes. Cloudbusters are a later development, emerging in the late 20th century, attributed to Don Croft and others. Evidence cited by proponents includes anecdotal accounts of improved weather, reduced chemtrail activity (a conspiracy theory involving alleged government spraying of chemicals from aircraft), and personal well-being. Mainstream science dismisses the concept of orgone energy and cloudbusters as pseudoscience. There is no scientific evidence to support the existence of orgone energy or the claims made about cloudbusters. Weather modification is a complex scientific field, and attributing changes to these devices is not supported by meteorological data or physics. The construction and deployment of cloudbusters are often associated with New Age spiritual practices and beliefs in environmental healing.
subtitle: Devices for Transforming Atmospheric Energy?

claims.claim: Cloudbusters can transform DOR into POR.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of improved weather and plant growth after deploying cloudbusters.
claims.criticism: Orgone energy is not recognized by mainstream science; no mechanism for such transformation exists.

claims.claim: Cloudbusters can mitigate the effects of electromagnetic radiation and chemtrails.
claims.evidence: Perceived reduction in chemtrail activity and improved personal health after using cloudbusters.
claims.criticism: Chemtrails are a conspiracy theory, and there's no scientific basis for cloudbusters affecting electromagnetic radiation.

claims.claim: Cloudbusters contribute to positive environmental changes.
claims.evidence: Observed improvements in local ecosystems and increased rainfall in drought-stricken areas.
claims.criticism: Weather patterns are complex and influenced by numerous factors; attributing changes solely to cloudbusters lacks scientific rigor.

keyFigures.name: Wilhelm Reich
keyFigures.role: Developed the concept of orgone energy.

keyFigures.name: Phil Callahan
keyFigures.role: Agricultural researcher who studied orgone and paramagnetism.

keyFigures.name: Scientists (unnamed)
keyFigures.role: Researchers who debunked the cloudbusting claims

timeline.event: Wilhelm Reich develops the theory of orgone energy.
The concept of cloudbusters emerges and is popularized.
Cloudbusters become a subject of interest in online communities and alternative health circles.

sources.author: Gardner, Martin
sources.work: Fads and Fallacies in the Name of Science
sources.title: Gardner, Martin
